figures.caption: Table 1: HPS-v2 win rate against the pretrained model on the HPS-v2 test prompt set for all setups (each at its reported checkpoint).
Figure 2: Curves of HPS-v2 win rate against the pretrained model for SFT (left), RWL (middle), and PPO (right).

Figure 3: End-of-training win-rate vs. baselines. Each subplot reports three paired comparisons: (gray-box) DiffCon vs. DiffCon-Naive; (white-box) DiffCon-J vs. LoRA; (white-box) DiffCon-S vs. LoRA. (a)-(c): HPSv2 win rates for SFT/RWL/PPO with orange error bars showing standard deviation; (d): human-evaluated win rate for PPO.
Table 5: Qualitative comparison of different methods using SFT training. We compare Pretrained sd v1.4, DiffCon, DiffCon-Naive, LoRA, DiffCon-J, and DiffCon-S.

Table 4: Side network variants in gray-box finetuning. Both methods share the same side-net backbone, but DiffCon uses a structured output (z, f) applied in the controller-form correction, while DiffCon-Naive directly adds an unsplit residual.

Table 2: Block definitions used in sθ. Common components: ResnetBlock2D: Standard ResNet block with GroupNorm, SiLU, and two Convolutional layers with kernel size 3× 3 and stride 1. Transformer2DModel: spatial transformer with cross-attention. Downsample2D: a convolutional layer with kernel size 3 × 3 and stride 2. Upsample2D: 2× Nearest Neighbor Resize + 3× 3 conv, stride 1.
Table 10: PPO evaluation results on HPS-v2 test prompt set at step 2400.

Figure 8: HPS-v2 win rate comparison between our polynomial reward weighting (LoRA, ours) and DPOKRWL (LoRA) during RWL finetuning. (a) uses teacher sampling where the images for training at each iteration are generated by the pretrained model, and (b) uses online sampling where the images for training are generated by the current diffusion policy.
Table 8: SFT evaluation results on HPS-v2 test prompt set at step 1000.
Table 9: RWL evaluation results on HPS-v2 test prompt set at step 2000.
Figure 6: Generations with different guidance strengths λmodel using RWL with DiffCon. Prompt: “A crazy looking fish swimming in Alcatraz in the style of artgerm”.

Figure 7: Generations with different guidance strengths λmodel using RWL with DiffCon-J. Prompt: “A cute humanoid cat soldier wears a yellow raincoat, carries a rifle, and ventures through a dense forest, looking back over their shoulder”.
Figure 9: Learning rate ablation for RWL and PPO on HPS-v2 win rate (comparing 1e-4 vs. 1e-5).
Figure 10: Ablation studies on τKL for PPO on HPS-v2 win rate (five learning curves correspond to τKL ∈ {0, 1e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3}). τKL =1e-4 leads to the best performance.
Table 11: Side network structure variants used in the ablation study.
Figure 12: RWL under the exponential reward weighting function wKL (Eq. (20)) with τRWL = 0.0015 on HPS-v2 win rate.
Figure 11: Ablation studies on τRWL for RWL (three HPS-v2 win rate curves in each subfigure correspond to τRWL ∈ {0.0005, 0.0025, 0.005}). τRWL = 0.0005 leads to the best performance.
Table 7: Qualitative comparison of different methods using PPO training. We compare Pretrained sd v1.4, DiffCon, DiffCon-Naive, LoRA, DiffCon-J, and DiffCon-S.
Figure 5: Generations with different guidance strengths λmodel using SFT with DiffCon-J. Prompt: “A bluejay is eating spaghetti”.

Figure 14: Ablation studies on the input of DiffCon for RWL, evaluated by the HPS-v2 win rate curve. The plot contains two curves corresponding to input mean=True or False, i.e., using the mean µ0(xt, c, t) or xt as the input to the side network sθ.
Figure 13: Ablation studies on DiffCon block structures evaluated by the HPS-v2 win rate curve. Each plot contains four curves corresponding to 1Res, 1Res+1Attn, 2Attn, and 2Res (main-paper setting).
Table 6: Qualitative comparison of different methods using RWL training. We compare Pretrained sd v1.4, DiffCon, DiffCon-Naive, LoRA, DiffCon-J, and DiffCon-S.
Figure 4: Generations with different guidance strengths λmodel using SFT with DiffCon. Prompt: “A black cat wearing a suit and smoking a cigar”.
